questions.q: What do all containers in a pod automatically share?
options: Network namespace (IP and localhost) and any explicitly mounted volumes
questions.explanation: Containers in a pod share the same network namespace — they all have the same IP and can communicate via localhost. They can also share storage volumes that are explicitly mounted in each container's spec. Filesystems are NOT shared unless a volume is mounted.

questions.q: Which multi-container pattern is used when a sidecar converts the main app's metrics format for Prometheus scraping?
questions.explanation: The Adapter pattern transforms the output of the main container into a standardised format. A common example is converting app-specific metrics into the Prometheus exposition format.

questions.q: How do you get logs from the 'log-agent' container in a multi-container pod named 'web-pod'?
questions.explanation: For multi-container pods, you must specify -c <container-name>. Without -c, kubectl logs fails with an error asking you to specify a container. Use --all-containers=true to get logs from all containers at once.

questions.q: Containers in the same pod can communicate with each other using localhost.
questions.explanation: True. All containers in a pod share the same network namespace, meaning they share the same IP address and port space. Container-to-container communication within a pod uses localhost.
